name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓمٓ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
```

```english
These are the signs of the wise Book, (2)
```

```arabic
هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣)
```

```english
a guidance and mercy for the virtuous, (3)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
```

```english
who maintain the prayer, pay the zakat, and are certain of the Hereafter. (4)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥)
```

```english
Those follow their Lord’s guidance, and it is they who are the felicitous. (5)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْتَرِى لَهْوَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٦)
```

```english
Among the people is he who buys diversionary talk that he may lead [people] astray from Allah’s way without any knowledge, and he takes it in derision. For such there is a humiliating punishment. (6)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا وَلَّىٰ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا كَأَنَّ فِىٓ أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْرًا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٧)
```

```english
When Our signs are recited to him he turns away disdainfully, as if he had not heard them [at all], as if there were a deafness in his ears. So inform him of a painful punishment. (7)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٨)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be gardens of bliss, (8)
```

```arabic
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
to remain in them [forever]—a true promise of Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (9)
```

```arabic
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ (١٠)
```

```english
He created the heavens without any pillars that you may see, and cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and He has scattered in it every kind of animal. And We sent down water from the sky and caused every splendid kind [of plant] to grow in it. (10)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذَا خَلْقُ ٱللَّهِ فَأَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ بَلِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (١١)
```

```english
This is the creation of Allah. Now show Me what others besides Him have created. Indeed, the wrongdoers are in manifest error! (11)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah, as suggested by its opening and conclusion and indicated by the general context (سياق) of its verses, is the call to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and to certainty (الإيقان) in the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment* — and the adherence to the universal principles of the laws of the religion (الدين).

It appears from the beginning of the Surah that it was revealed concerning some of the polytheists who used to hinder people from listening to the Quran by spreading embellished, distracting tales (أحاديث مزوقة ملهية). This is mentioned in the reports regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of His saying: "And among the people is he who buys idle talk to mislead from the way of Allah," and the relevant narration will follow. Thus, the Surah was revealed to clarify the fundamental doctrines of the religion and the universal principles of its true laws, and it recounted something of the story of Luqman the Wise and his exhortations as a counterpoint to their distracting tales.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses. Among the most luminous verses (غرر الآيات) within it is His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الْباطِلُ** "That is because Allah is the Real, and what they invoke besides Him is falsehood" (31:30).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ هُدىً وَرَحْمَةً لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ** — until His saying — **يُوقِنُونَ** "**These are the signs of the Wise Book, a guidance and mercy for the virtuous** — until — **who are certain of the Hereafter**" (31:2-4). The explanation of the vocabulary of these verses has preceded in the previous Surahs.

The Book has been described as "Wise" (الحكيم) to signal that it contains nothing of "idle talk" (لهو الحديث); rather, it is a Book in which there is no fracture (انثلام) through which idle talk or false speech could enter. He also described it as "guidance" (هدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and "mercy" (رحمة) for the virtuous (المحسنين) to complete the description of its wisdom. Thus, it guides to the true reality and leads one to it, unlike idle talk which distracts man from what concerns him; and it is a mercy, not a vengeance that diverts one from blessing.

He described the virtuous (المحسنين) as those who maintain the prayer (الصلاة) and give the zakat (الزكاة), which are the two pillars of deeds, and as those who have certainty in the Hereafter (الآخرة). This certainty necessitates monotheism (التوحيد), the message (الرسالة), and general godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against sin*. All of this stands in contrast to the Book being opposed to the idle talk that is listened to by those who attend to such distractions.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَها هُزُواً** "**Among the people is he who buys idle talk that he may mislead [others] from Allah’s way without knowledge, and take it in derision**" (31:6) etc. Idle talk (لهو) is that which distracts you from what concerns you. "Idle talk" (لهو الحديث) refers to speech that distracts from the truth by its very nature, such as superstitious myths and stories that invite corruption and immorality, or by what accompanies it, such as singing poetry or using musical instruments (الملاهي), flutes (المزامير), and stringed instruments (المعازف); all of this is included in "idle talk."

The requirement of the context for His saying: **لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** "that he may mislead [others] from Allah’s way without knowledge" is that "the way of Allah" (سبيل الله) refers to the Noble Quran with the true doctrinal and practical knowledge it contains, especially the stories of the Prophets and their past nations. This is because idle talk and embellished, fabricated myths first oppose these stories and then demolish the foundation of all other true knowledge, weakening them in the eyes of the people.

This is supported by His subsequent saying: **وَيَتَّخِذَها هُزُواً** "and take it in derision." For idle talk, being a form of discourse, first opposes the [divine] discourse and treats it with mockery.

Thus, the meaning of "the way of Allah" is the Quran with its stories and knowledge. It is as if the intent of the one who "buys idle talk" is to mislead people by turning them away from the Quran and to take the Quran in derision by claiming it is just talk like his own, and myths like his myths.

His saying: **بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** "without knowledge" relates to "mislead" (يضل). It is, in reality, a description of the misguidance of those who are led astray, rather than just the act of misleading by the misleaders, even though the misleaders themselves also possess no knowledge. Then He threatened them by His saying: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** "**For such there is a humiliating punishment**"—that is, a debasing punishment that weakens and humiliates them in exchange for their arrogance (استكبار) in the world.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا وَلَّى مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها كَأَنَّ فِي أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْراً** "**When Our signs are recited to him, he turns away disdainfully as if he had not heard them, as if there were a deafness in his ears**" (31:7) etc. This is a description of that person who buys idle talk to mislead people from the Quran and mocks it. Heaviness (الوقر) refers to a heavy load, and the meaning of "heaviness in his ears" is as if they are plugged with something that prevents hearing. It is also said to be a metaphor for deafness (الصمم).

The meaning is: when Our signs—meaning the Quran—are recited to this buyer of idle talk, he turns away and withdraws in arrogance (مستكبرا), as if he had never heard them at all, as if he were deaf; so give him the tidings of a painful punishment.

The pronoun referring back to "he who buys" (من يشتري) was initially singular, as in "buys" (يشتري), "to mislead" (ليضل), and "take it" (يتخذها), in consideration of the literal word (اللفظ). Then the pronoun became plural in consideration of the meaning (المعنى), and then returned to the singular in consideration of the literal word, as in "to him" (عليه) and others; so it has been said. It is also possible that the pronoun "for them" (لهم) in the previous verse refers to the collective of the misleader and the misled indicated by the context, while the pronouns referring back to "who" (من) remain singular throughout.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ النَّعِيمِ** — until His saying — **الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** "**Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them are gardens of bliss** — until — **the All-mighty, the All-wise**" (31:8-9). This is a return, after warning that buyer and threatening him with a humiliating and then a painful punishment, to giving glad tidings to the virtuous (المحسنين) and comforting them with the eternal Garden of Bliss promised by Him, the Exalted; and His promise is the truth.

Since the goal of the one who bought idle talk was to confuse the matter for those he misleads without knowledge—so they might think the Quran is among the false myths like his own myths, and he treats it with contempt and pays no heed to the verses recited to him in arrogance, which is a disparagement of Allah, the Glorified—He first emphasized what He promised the virtuous by His saying: **وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا** "the promise of Allah is true." Secondly, He described Himself with absolute might (العزة), so that no humiliation or contempt can affect Him, and with absolute wisdom (الحكمة), so that no falsehood, jest, or superstition can enter His speech.

Thirdly, He described Himself as the One who regulates the affairs of the heavens, the earth, plants, animals, and humans because He is their Creator. Therefore, He has the right to promise these the Garden and those the punishment. This is His saying: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** "**He created the heavens without any pillars that you may see**" (31:10) etc.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** "**He created the heavens without any pillars that you may see**" etc. It has preceded in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying: **اللهُ الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** "It is Allah who raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see" (13:2) that His saying "that you may see" (ترونها) could be an explanatory clause, meaning: you see them and they have no pillars. Or it could be a restrictive clause, meaning: He created them without *visible* pillars, signaling that there are pillars that are not seen.

His saying: **وَأَلْقى فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ** "and He cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you." That is, He placed towering mountains in it so that it does not tremble with you. In this, there is an indication that there is a direct link between mountains and earthquakes.

His saying: **وَبَثَّ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ دابَّةٍ** "and He scattered in it every kind of animal." That is, He spread across the earth every animal that crawls upon it.

His saying: **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ** "and We sent down water from the sky and caused every splendid kind [of plant] to grow in it." That is, We sent down water—which is rain—from the direction of the heights, and We caused to grow in it something of every noble (كريم) plant pair that possesses benefits and uses. In this, there is an allusion to the sexuality of plants, and the discussion regarding this has preceded in similar verses.

The shift (الالتفات) from the third person to the first person plural ("We") is to indicate the perfection of care for His affair, as has been said.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **هذا خَلْقُ اللهِ فَأَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ بَلِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ** "**This is the creation of Allah. Now show Me what others besides Him have created. Rather the wrongdoers are in manifest error**" (31:11). When He showed them His creation and His regulation (تدبير) of the heavens, the earth, and what is upon them—thereby establishing His Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية)—He challenged them to show Him anything created by their gods, if they were indeed gods and lords. If they are unable to show anything, His oneness (وحدانية) in His Divinity and Lordship is established.

He only challenged them to show something created by their gods—while they admit that creation belongs to Allah alone and do not attribute creation to their gods, but only attribute regulation (التدبير) to them—because He attributed to Allah a creation that is identical to regulation without any separation. If their gods had any regulation in the world, they would have the creation of what they regulate. Since they have no creation, they have no regulation; thus, there is no god but Allah and no lord besides Him.

The verse is framed as a speech from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) because this type of address—"Now show Me what others besides Him have created"—is not appropriate coming from anyone other than him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ** "And among the people is he who buys idle talk" was revealed concerning al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn 'Alqamah ibn Kaldah—ibn 'Abd al-Dar ibn Qusay ibn Kilab. He was a merchant who would travel to Persia and buy the stories of the non-Arabs (الأعاجم) and narrate them to the Quraysh, saying to them: "Muhammad tells you the stories of 'Ad and Thamud, and I tell you the stories of Rustam and Isfandiyar and the chronicles of the Chosroes." So they would listen to his talk and abandon listening to the Quran. This is narrated from al-Kalbi.

**I say**: This meaning was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Bayhaqi from Ibn 'Abbas. It is not unlikely that this was the reason for the revelation of the entire Surah, as previously indicated.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from Yahya ibn 'Abadah from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): I said: [Regarding] the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ** "And among the people is he who buys idle talk," he (peace be upon him) said: "Among it is singing (الغناء)."

**I say**: This meaning was narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Mehran from him (peace be upon him), and with its chain from al-Washsha' from Imam al-Rida from him (peace be upon him), and with its chain from al-Hasan ibn Harun from him (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I heard him saying: "Singing is among that for which Allah has promised the Fire," and he recited this verse: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَها هُزُواً ـ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** "And among the people is he who buys idle talk to mislead from the way of Allah without knowledge and take it in derision; for such there is a humiliating punishment."

In the same source, with its chain from Abu Basir, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the earnings of female singers. He said: "That which men enter upon is forbidden (حرام), but that which is invited to weddings is not problematic. And that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ** 'And among the people is he who buys idle talk to mislead from the way of Allah.'"

In *al-Majma'*: Abu Umamah narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: "It is not permissible to teach female singers, nor to sell them, and their price is forbidden (حرام). The confirmation of that has been revealed in the Book of Allah: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ** 'And among the people is he who buys idle talk...'"

**I say**: It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* by a large number of compilers of the collections from Abu Umamah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source (*al-Majma'*): It is narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "It is the slandering of the truth and mocking it. It is what Abu Jahl and his companions used to bring when he said: 'O assembly of Quraysh, shall I not feed you from the Zaqqum with which your companion threatens you?' Then he sent for butter and dates and said: 'This is the Zaqqum with which he threatens you.'" He (peace be upon him) said: "And among it is singing."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi al-Dunya extracted from 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) who said: "A nation in which the lute (البربط) is present is not sanctified."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the report of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: **وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ ـ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** "And among the people is he who buys idle talk to mislead from the way of Allah without knowledge," he said: "He is al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn 'Alqamah ibn Kaldah, from the tribe of 'Abd al-Dar ibn Qusay. Al-Nadr was a narrator of people's stories and their poems. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا وَلَّى مُسْتَكْبِراً** 'And when Our signs are recited to him, he turns away disdainfully...'"

In the same source, from his father from al-Husayn ibn Khalid from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him): I said to him: "Inform me about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ** 'By the heaven with its tracks' (51:7)." He said: "It is woven (محبوكة) to the earth," and he interlaced his fingers. I said: "How can it be woven to the earth when Allah says: **رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** 'He raised the heavens without any pillars that you may see'?" He said: "Glory be to Allah! Does He not say: **بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها** 'without any pillars that you may see'?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Then there are pillars, but you do not see them."
